title: Privatisation, nationalisation & regulation
summary:
## Intervening in markets with market power

Where firms have **monopoly/market power**, governments use **competition policy, regulation, privatisation and nationalisation** to protect consumers and improve efficiency. This chapter weighs public vs private ownership and how to control natural monopolies.

## Privatisation

**Privatisation** = transferring assets/industries from the **public to the private sector**.
- **Arguments for:** the profit motive raises **productive/dynamic efficiency** and cuts X-inefficiency; competition and choice; revenue for government; frees firms from political interference.
- **Arguments against:** private **natural monopolies** may exploit consumers (higher prices); loss of important **externalities/public-service goals**; short-termism; one-off sale revenue; possible job losses.

## Nationalisation

**Nationalisation** = bringing industries into **state ownership**.
- **For:** control of **natural monopolies** in the public interest, capture of positive externalities, universal service, economies of scale, protection of strategic industries.
- **Against:** **X-inefficiency** and lack of profit incentive, political interference, funding burden on taxpayers, ‘moral hazard’ of guaranteed survival.

## Regulation of natural monopolies

A **natural monopoly** (huge economies of scale, e.g. water, rail track) can't be broken up efficiently, so it is **regulated** instead. Tools:
- **Price-cap regulation (‘RPI − X’)** — caps price rises below inflation to force efficiency gains onto consumers.
- **Profit/rate-of-return regulation**, quality standards, and performance targets.
- **Yardstick competition** — comparing regional monopolies against each other.
*Problems:* **regulatory capture** (the regulator serves the firm), information asymmetry (the firm knows its true costs), and setting X too tight/loose.

## Competition policy

Governments/agencies act to promote competition and protect consumers:
- **Controlling mergers** that would create excessive market power.
- **Prohibiting anti-competitive practices** — cartels, price-fixing, predatory pricing, collusion.
- **Promoting contestability** by lowering barriers to entry and deregulating.
- **Consumer protection** laws.

## Evaluation

The right approach depends on the **industry** (is it a natural monopoly?), the **strength of competition**, and the risk of **government/regulatory failure**. Ownership matters less than the **degree of competition and the quality of regulation** — a key evaluative theme.

## Why it matters

This chapter turns the market-structures theory of Unit 7 into real policy. It is rich ground for evaluation essays on efficiency, consumer welfare and the proper role of the state.

keyTerms.definition: Transferring ownership of assets or industries from the public to the private sector.
Bringing privately-owned industries into state ownership.
An industry where economies of scale make a single supplier the lowest-cost provider.
Limiting a monopoly's price increases to below inflation to force efficiency gains to consumers.
When a regulator comes to act in the interest of the firms it regulates rather than consumers.
Government measures to promote competition and prevent abuse of market power.
Removing barriers to entry and rules to increase competition and contestability.

diagrams: **Natural monopoly:** a downward-sloping LRAC over the whole market with demand; show that one firm minimises cost, and mark the loss-making point if forced to price at P = MC (justifying regulation/subsidy).
**Monopoly vs regulated price:** compare the profit-max (MC=MR) price with a regulated price closer to allocative efficiency.
tips: Argue that **the degree of competition and regulation often matters more than ownership** — a strong evaluative line.
Use the **natural monopoly diagram** to justify why some industries are regulated rather than broken up.
Explain **RPI − X price caps** and their weakness: **regulatory capture** and **information asymmetry**.
Give **balanced privatisation/nationalisation** cases (efficiency & revenue vs externalities & consumer exploitation).
Bring in real examples (**UK privatisations of BT, British Gas and rail; water regulation by Ofwat; or China's state-owned enterprises**) for application.

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Clear on privatisation, regulation and competition policy.
